speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And my mother forgot that this afterthought child of hers was born in Nez Perce County because her other four were born in Latah County. So she registered our births in Boise, the state capital, as having all been born in Later County. And when I discovered this, I pointed out the error. We wrote the state capital and found out it would take an act of God, even though mother and the nurse that attended her were still living to get that date or that location of my birth changed. So that's the way it is in Boise. Since I was born in 1906. I have many early day memories, and since I was a later child in the family, I spent much time alone with my parents, which meant that I adopted or by osmosis or capillary attraction or what have you. Acquired a great deal of their thinking so that I feel very at home in this period.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I loved my home and I loved my family, and we all loved our friends, so that there was great empathy that went on around our table, on the front steps that led to the kitchen or in the back steps that led to the kitchen over any task that was shared. And since the help that was employed in the home was always a neighbor or a neighbor's child, or somebody who homesteaded next door, or somebody who was connected in some way by place with where we lived. We were very polite to them, and they were very polite to us, and we were on an equal or nearly so social basis. I'd like to talk a little bit about the Indians. I have known. One of them in particular was my father's landlord. His name was Stephen Rubin, and he was a full blood who had inherited or been given by the government allotted, I suppose I should say, a large tract of land which was across the canyon from our house, but adjacent to my father's land, which was also across the canyon from where we lived.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: The Rubin Ranch was a choice place, and father ran it for many years. It was distinguished by having a large orchard that I don't have any idea who planted, but it certainly wasn't an Indian because they weren't along in our culture enough to provide in that way for fruit. But someone had planted a large orchard there, and the Indians would come and pick fruit from time to time. Particularly this was and they were unobtrusive and didn't bother anybody. But I remember one day a lone rider came up the road from the Clearwater River and down through our gate and down our driveway, and passed the house and passed the barn, and out through the corral and down across the canyon, over to the Reuben Ranch. Now, I watched him the whole way with my father's good field glasses, because I was a board little girl and had lots of time on my hands.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: So anything that was moving that was a little out of the ordinary got full attention. And he went over to the Reuben ranch and no one was there. So he took the wood box, emptied all of the contents on the kitchen floor, tamped down a little, and went out and filled the wood box with apples. Then it was a little large to put on the back of his saddle, and a little large to balance on the front, so he had great difficulty in getting it back and on horseback to where he wanted to go. But I watched this procedure and was a little amused because, of course, mother interpreted what had happened, and I don't know how many apples he got home with, but that made a very entertained interlude for me. The Reubens were a fine family, and Stephen Rubin was an ordained Presbyterian minister. So when he came to dinner, on went the white tablecloth and a good meal was provided.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I mean, accompany Sunday dinner. And the Reverend Stephen Reuben always said, Grace. We were accustomed to grace when our grandfather came, but not when our dad presided. Now, another Indian whose land border just on the north was Eddie Conner. Eddie, I think, also was a full blooded Indian and a handsome, large man who had a very round head. And at one time I embarrassed my mother greatly by saying to Mr. Conner, Mr. Conner, what makes your head so round? And of course he laughed and mother laughed. But she could have killed me. Eddie Conner performed a nice service for us later in life, because there was a suit brought regarding the boundary lines of the home place, and it was said that we had 40ft of someone else's land. The full west border of the place, which was on a straight line and which had a few locust trees on it, and several walnut trees that dad had planted along our driveway. Those trees and Mr. Conners testimony saved that 40ft strip of land over this long space for our home place. It seems that the law recognizes a border if it has living growth on it, that shows length of time.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And since both the walnut and locust trees were of large size, they knew that that land had been paid taxes upon and farmed by the owner of our place, who was my father, Walter F chain, for all those many years. Also, Mr. Conner testified at the trial that he had known my father for some years, and that indeed, in all that time Walter had farmed this land. I have another tiny story about the locust trees. One time, at an early period when dad and mother were out working in the garden, they finished what they were doing, and dad put a case knife up in the crotch of one of the trees. Now, a case knife is a full sized dinner knife with a wooden handle. It was used by early day bachelors and that sort of thing, but it never was used in serving food at my mother's table. However, I suppose that's the kind of cutlery that my father had when he was a bachelor before he was married. At any rate, it was in the family possession and was the handy tool that day. Well, no one came along to finish whatever the job had been going on. And so the knife stayed in the tree, and in due time the tree grew around it and further completely covered it.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And I have tried to find it since. There is not one blemish on the trunk of that tree, which still 60 years later is right there and the knife is completely gone, incorporated in the middle of that tree. Dad took great pride in his orchard, and all of us savored the delicious fruit that came to our table from time to time. Dad felt that fruit or vegetables were to be had in their season, and he gave very little time to winter strawberries or winter tomatoes. What he thought was good was what was picked and brought immediately into the table. And indeed, there never was a better taste when it was accompanied by a homemade, well done butter and good thick separated cream. That was ambrosia. In his archery he had early peaches and fall peaches, silver prunes and plums and purple prunes. He had peach plums, which I had seen out an exorbitant price in the market and which we didn't care for. And there were two trees of. So the children were put to taking these to the pigs. I never see them on the market at $0.50 for about six, that I don't have a little of my hair curled.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Dad also had summer apples, fall apples and winter apples. And how we did enjoy those. Mother made wonderful sauce. And we also had three kinds of black cherries Lamberts, Bings and Black Republicans. And since we ourselves were black Republicans, those who had special. In that garden was an enormous patch of red raspberries. These were the bane of the women's existence, because they never could keep all of the drafted things picked in season. And when my brother finally brought his bride there, she thought she never had such a burden placed upon her. But it had, in the middle of this patch, a bare place which was wrought by a large young man in probably about 15 named William Nixon, whose father was and mother were very old friends of the family, and who came to pick raspberries with the family and.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Who sat down in a convenient area and pulled the raspberry vines to convenient distance and filled his pail. Of course, the patch never recovered from his picking, but we always called it the William Nixon Bare spot, and it remained there as long as the patch did. My dad was a great homemaker. He not only planted those black walnuts which still lined the driveway long since unused, but he had lovely roses along there too. And also from time to time, beautiful patches of strawberries. I remember one time when he had a dear friend whose husband was a pioneer, John Hall. He had John and Lila Hall in that garden, and bet Mrs. Hall that no matter what, he could find a strawberry too big for her to consume in one bite and not have juice run from the corner of her mouth. Well, now, Mrs. Hall was a very beautiful woman who took great pride in her appearance and whom we all dearly loved. But she took his bet, and he found a in an enormous berry, which she promptly popped in her mouth, and down the corner of each side came the red marker of her defeat.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: We also had blackberries from time to time, but they were such space pigs and took such a lot of room and were so difficult to pick that dad eliminated them. The garden was a great source of pleasure to father, who, as a pioneer boy, deeply cherished good food. And when guests came to see us, which was nearly every Sunday in good weather, he loved to load their rigs. Now it wasn't a buggy and it wasn't a wagon, and it wasn't a model T, whatever. And but he came to our house in was rig and he loved to load up their rigs with delicious things from the garden. It swelled his heart to be able to do it. He felt just warm and loving and provident and so rich when he could do this. And as long as I can remember my father as a host, he continued to do this even when he retired to town and kept Jersey cows because they were fun. He told me one time I liked to go up to the pool hall and play a little solo with my best friends, and maybe win a hickey and bring you home a chocolate bar, and then go out to the barn and feed something.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: He had Jersey cows because their milk content was so hot there. Cream content was so high, and there was just one criterion about a good jersey. She had to have long enough tits so a man could get his hands on him, because he damned if he wanted to have that milk sprayed all over the lower part of his palm. When he tried to milk a short headed cow, he wouldn't have her. Dad and his dogs were great pleasure to me. To the first dog in the family that I remember was a character. I, the story goes, was a baby in my cradle on the south porch, and my arm was just long enough so that my hand could poke out between the slats of the side of the cradle, and mother, working in the kitchen with the door open between us, heard a little thump and a little sigh, and looked out to find a most engaging black dog with white around his muzzle and a weariness to his body, a warning to the pads of his feet contentedly licking the hand of the baby.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: They named him Jack and figured that probably he had grown so tired he no longer could follow the covered wagon which had gone along the road that morning. I am sure the family that lost him were sore of heart, but he was our great, great good friend from then till when he died, which was many years later. The trouble with Jack was that he liked to roam at night, and the neighbors didn't care for his visits. So the man on the north who was farming the Connor place shot him in the right, left in the right hind leg, and somebody we never know who shot him in the see. It was the left hind leg in the right foreleg, so poor Jack had to hobble round in great misery, and no one ever thought of taking out those bullets. They just let Jack get along, and he licked those wounds from then until he died. And of course, must have suffered terrible pain. These days we'd have him to the vet in a minute, but those days to take an animal to the vet meant that he had to be an animal that paid his way. And of course, Jack was the best doc dog in the world.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: But after he became impaired, he was a beloved pensioner. Along with him was stub, named for his short bobbed tail. He did all the work after Jack was incapacitated. And since my father always had cattle to some extent, a little jag of cattle, they were called. Then he would be the mainstay for corralling them, for gathering them, for a drive, for just bringing them home at night. A man with stock can't get along without a good dog, because if he does, he wastes an amazing amount of time. Another dog we had that was my father's dear friend was Missy. She was an Australian collie and a spade female, and she accompanied us always to the toilet, which was out to the north of the house. And as we went to and from a nice little dog, let us know of her presence by leaping and touching with her cold nose in very gentle but insistent fashion, our right elbows.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Of course, she got many a pat and all kinds of lovely visiting, both going and coming, and we enjoyed it and she enjoyed it. My father, it fell to his lot to have to shoot each of his dogs when they became so old that they had had to leave this world, and it was kinder to do it thus. And each time it was a major, major operation for him, and one which he he could hardly bear to do. But he was always stern with himself, as with the necessities of life. One time when I was a little girl during World War one, and my father had returned to the farm because my big brothers, who had been running the farm and who were about 19 and 21, almost had been were being occupied by World War one. And so we went back to the farm to grow food. And during that time, my dad came home from riding down on the river, and he found along the way a little Kerr puppy, and it had been put out of a sack, I suppose, for somebody figuring that it would die and they wouldn't have to have the pain of putting it to death.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And so they put it out to starve to death or a coyote to get it. So, dad, of course, he couldn't stand that. So he gathered it up and tenderly brought it home to his little girl. I thought it was a yummy. It was the sweetest little old pup, as every puppy is. And so I carried it every place I went. And I'd have had it in bed with me if mother would have allowed. But that certainly was beyond the pale. We had no animals in the house, none whatever ever, though we dearly loved them and had them as part of our lives. Just outside the door, this little dog and I had a traumatic experience. I carried it, as I said, every place I went. And one night I was feeding the chickens and I had a big pan of feed, and I was holding it in my left hand with the doggie balanced on the pan. And then with my right hand, I screwed the wheat and the dog became a patient with this and jumped or fell to the ground. I stumbled over the little soft belly, and my foot came right down on it and broke its back.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh, I thought I'd die. But that was one of those things we learned on a farm that you never think too much of an animal or anything, because you have to kill your own violence. We had rattlesnakes on this place so that everywhere a child's foot was set down in wheat, in weeds, in grasses, in any growth, you watched where your foot went. This was also helpful so that you didn't stay up in a car park. But in the main it was to protect us from snakes. I learned to tell a rattler from a bull snake, which was protected, and a rattler which was not. We were given great instruction, very firm instruction as to what to do. When we found a rattler, we were to send somebody in the party if there was one to the house, while someone who had spotted the snake kept it in a vision. And that way a big person could come with a hole, the 22 or an ax and dispatch the snake. We were never to let it out of our sight. And then in that area, we were to be exceedingly wary for the next several days, because the mate would come to hunt its partner and would be found in the area.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: This happened invariably… apparently snakes are monogamous and made for life. Now, I don't know whether this is true or not, but certainly we got snake after snake after snake because it came to follow its dead partner. We found one right at the door of the barn, coiled ready. We found two that I know of in the raspberry patch. One time, years later, in another canyon, working for my brother. My husband had a snake slither down the handle of his hay fork as he pitched a bundle into the wagon. Of course, these snakes were complete enemies and no one protected them ever. There were good snakes, of course, who killed black snakes and and water snakes and harmless garter snakes and so forth that were protected because they killed varmints to. But for the deadly snake, we were very, very busy dispatching them the same in the bird world. We were taught the good ones and the bad ones. We had large flocks of pigeons, and my mother made delicious pigeon pot pie. And these pigeons, however, were dirty and creatures, and father had to finally kill them all off because they they roost high.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: They are very prolific and their droppings is very bad for hey, animals don't like to eat it after it's been polluted and their droppings on machinery is damaging, and they have the poorest of domestic instincts in that. They'll lay their eggs with 1 or 2 sticks on a two before a shelf, just a stud going along in a shed, so they don't bother much about their eggs rolling off or their young rolling off. An amazing number of them didn't, but and the birds were beautiful as they as they flew and beautiful as they walked. There's nothing prettier than than the streamline of a lovely pigeon. And their coloring is out of this world. So many varieties and such a lovely sound to their wings and to their cooing. We love them. And dad had them first in a coat, but they outgrew that and short order, and then took over first the machine shed and then went into the barn.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And when they began to destroy the hay, then dad had to take action. Now we had zillions of of swallows who built their sweet little mud nests around. We had lots of bluebirds in those days and and yellow canaries, and we loved them and protected them in every way we could. But the English sparrow began to invade, and that sparrow was really a destructive animal. It was almost as bad as a as a. Magpie, both of which are predators of other animals nests, and both usurp the homes that are made by other birds, kill their young, scratch off their eggs, and take the places themselves we were taught to destroy, if possible, both species of birds. A hawk now was a somewhat protected animal, particularly if it was a large hawk, a red head, or a barn hawk. They sailed so beautifully in the clouds against the wind. Sometimes they couldn't make any headway at all with the breeze they tried to go against. And those amazing eyes of those creatures, way up there in the air, would see the movement of a mole, or a tiny squirrel, or a little mouse way down in the hog pasture and zoom they come down and have it.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Now, a sparrowhawk was another thing. We killed sparrowhawks religiously. That's what the 22 was used most for to do hit a sparrowhawk square in the eye. That was the goal because the sparrowhawk ate baby chickens. We had a little flurry with our pigs to our pigs were in a pasture north of the house, out back of the toilet and off to themselves where the their noise or their odors didn't distress the household. They were great snake killers. Apparently the layer of fat undress pigs skin protects him from the venom because they could kill rattlers or any other kind of snakes, and they were great to help keep down the population of snakes. But one time we found a kind of of pig that was new to us. It was a Missouri Razorback that had been shipped in in a government project, and those Razorbacks were indeed high along their backbone, and they had hair straight up along that area.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Now, our poll in China is in do Rock. Reds were nice round, flat back to pigs and they were domesticated and we could live with them nicely. But those wretched Razorbacks ate mother's chickens. And I tell you, it was a chicken's life if it got into the hog pasture. So we couldn't get rid of those creatures fast enough. The only time a red saw or a black saw. Oh, we had a few. New Hampshire's two with the white band around the middle. The only time those were dangerous was when one of their little pigs got caught in the fence. Now a baby pig is tiny, like any other animal for its species. When it's little, when it's young. And so it will go through a crack in the fence, and at due time it grows and it gets so big it gets stuck in the hole that it's been using all this time, and it'll squeal, oh, it's being squeezed. It doesn't like it. And it raises the racket. And the mother cell gets very excited, and she nips the pig to increase its efforts.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And if she draws blood, which she easily can, she becomes very excited. And she'll she'll just choose a pig up to death. And mother, being a town girl, didn't know about all this, but she was game. And she took the ax one day and got in a pig pen and fought off two and raged sows who had a pig that they were just dispatching. And she took the ax with her. I remember seeing that ax flailing and mother's apron flying, and her determined, beautiful little face set in lines of complete combat, and she was flailing away at the hogs with the sharp end of the ax, meaning, of course, to hit them with a dull and. But she got a little confused. Well, anyway, she scared off the women both feet and saved the piglet. But often my mother didn't often take any hand in the operation of farm. However, outside the house, never. She never gathered an egg. She never picked a pea. She sometimes did fruit, but not other things. Excuse me?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: The calves or something, because that could be done on horseback. But to a woman there were seven days of work because on Sunday she had company in good weather and in bad weather. It was it was not suitable to go any place in the rain or snow or cold. The other thing she said was that a female animal, before giving birth, or even before conception, was given a little time to rest and be ready for her in her chores. But a woman had her children when she was depleted physically and conceived most easily. She thought the reason for this was nature's way of perpetuating the race. If this female was going to die, it could surely reproduce first, I guess. Is it ready? Yes. By weakest. I think she felt that she understood it as the fact that she seemed to conceive when she was, in her words, run down when she was tired and least physically fit to handle the business of having a child.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Then was when she conceived, and she had her first three children in about five years, less than five years. Then there was a space of three, and then there was the space of four, and she prevented. She felt conception by nursing her children over long, because as long as she nursed a child, she didn't conceive. And of course, in those days of no restrictions of any kind… reproduction… a woman just lived as best she could. And my father said to me one time, I never felt anything but a welcome for one of my children. Well, now, where children just came, whether they were planned for or invited, then I think that was quite a statement of character on his part. And it's been greatly reassuring to me because certainly as number five, when it took my mother 48 hours to bring me into the world with the longest and most painful of deliveries, I would not have known life.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Had it been a planned family, I have known more than one offspring. That was the flower of the flock that was born late. Later. My mother, of course, was just 30 when I came. But there is an illustrious family in Moscow whose name I shall not give, because I'm going to tell some secrets. And this dear lady was to have her fifth child, and she did not want it… but here she was, pregnant again, in the family way they called it. Then. Well, she went with the club of her nice lady friends here in Moscow. On a picnic down to Kendrick. She climbed a tree and jumped off. She did it repeatedly. She thought maybe she'd miscarried. Miscarried. And in this day and age, where abortion is supposed to be fully accepted, I do want to say that the Lord kept that baby for her, and he went on to be probably as illustrious a son as this community has ever produced, having later been president of the Ford Foundation, Dean of the Business School at Harvard, and presently lives in New York, and I speak of… him only with admiration. And I tell this story only because one of her peers told it to me as an illustration of what nature does for the human family when there is no planning.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I would like to go back for a moment and talk about my mother. As a lonely woman on the farm. She could see no light in any window of another white woman from her house. She could see way across the Clearwater River over toward Rubens and look out. And sometimes we could see a light from over there. And with the field glasses we could see distinguished buildings. But the Reuben ranch house was was hidden from ours by the trees. And besides that, it was never inhabited by any one but a hired cook during harvest. No Indian woman ever lived there, and no white woman ever lived there. During my memory. This had occurred earlier, but not during my memory. Up north of us on the corner place. From time to time there would be a woman, but the house was just up around a bend, so it could not be seen from our place. And mother was accustomed to daily company. Her father had had a grocery store and meat market, and later a factory back in Gladwin, Michigan, before they migrated west. She was accompanied to a custom to people.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: So the other day I ran on to a catalog which was, I think, called the Jolly Jokers. No, that isn't the correct name. I'll hunt it up on these days. This catalog was of a club of lonely people who wished to correspond with each other over the whole surface of the globe, and so mother joined it. It cost a big, fat $10. But she joined the club and geography came alive for us children, because there was Mr. Jones down in Quito, Ecuador, who sent her gifts and sent her interesting letters. And the gifts would be such things as the carving of the local water carrier, or a lovely embroidered doily that if enough such doilies were put together, a pretty hand embroidered hat could be made or such. This was sent as a sample of the handwork of the Indian women of the area. Or there might have been a girl whose last name was Magnus daughter, and she was from Denmark, and she sent mother a piece of hand-woven linen, which I still have, and which is as heavy as heavy, almost as foil for for thickness and.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Strength of texture. Or there was a young man in Tunis. There were various people who sent her the sands of local rivers, so that she had an enormous collection of the sands of the rivers of the world, so that we knew about the Dnieper, we knew about the Amazon, we knew about the game cheese. We knew about any of the Marne. We knew about the rivers and the surface of the earth, because mother had little sample of the sand that had been right taken from right alongside that flowing water. This was delightful for us all. And it made the mail such an important thing, because scarcely any time did dad get on horseback and go to town, because there was no rural delivery in the early time, and get the mail and inside would be all of these delights. My mother's mind was always alert and curious and enjoying, and no matter if it was a stray of any kind who tied his horse at our front gate and came up to be fed. Because of course, you fed any human being who came to your door, because otherwise they'd have to go hungry because there were no restaurants around, of course, in easy access.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: So anytime somebody needed bed or board, any human being gave it to them, and you gave them what you had. And if it was very limited, that was never questioned. And scarcely did anyone ever offer to pay. This was beside the point. The point was that mother would have somebody to visit with for a little while, and no matter who it was. There were some that she just couldn't find much of interest in, but mostly mother could find something to visit with in this traveling person. And we usually housed the school teacher who taught halfway down, the coyote graded what they later named Rattlesnake College, and she usually had 1 or 2 hired men at her table. Her hot cake griddle could bake six large cakes at a time. And of course, the main staple of any good working man's breakfast was something that stuck to his ribs, called a good hot cake that always had smoked cured meats. Or would it be bacon or ham? Hotcakes and fried eggs were breakfast with lots of homemade sirup, and that was a good enough meal for anybody. And all the people could work on it, go to school on it, and starve to death on it.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: If her name was Lila Jane. And that's the way we lived. As for diet in the winter times, I knew at the time that it was difficult for mother. But now that I'm a grown person, how much more I empathize with the maladjustment in which she lived and how hard it must have been on my father, who held her dear and had to witness all this, and at the same time never could have a shoulder to shoulder companion who earned with him with eggs and butter and produce, and who also was a constant drain for doctor's bills. I'd like to talk a little more about the Indians I have known, because I left that subject just over from our farm was popular. Now I have three P's in the pronunciation of his name, because that's the way I was taught. He was an Indian man who came up to his 80. That's 80 acres allotted by the government with his two squaws each summer, his place at the Connor Place and the Reuben Place, like all Indian homes, had a door yard utterly alive with hollyhocks, which only allowed for tramped down paths to and from the door to the house, and to the toilet and to the barn.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Otherwise they grew all over the place. And and hollyhocks to me are Miss Flowers Pyo Patel. Now it's spelled these days, so it's pop. Oh, a l k t. But we said popular Patel. However it was, he came up each summer, and once in a while he'd come and visit with my dad, who was great friends of all the Indians. Another Indian who came to visit us was Charlie White. He was a half breed and a handsome, large man who was a great baseball catcher in later times and played in the same team with my husband, who was a kid from Gifford and at 16 was a hired second baseman. Charlie played for free with the Lap or Spalding teams on 4th of July and and stock shows and such celebrations as would come through the warm weather. And my husband, Guy Wicks, would be hired to come down and give him $20 on the 4th of July to beef up the excellence of the hitting staff.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Charlie Charlie's background. Is illustrative of the scarcity of white women who came west. Men were more adventurous and then they came first. And then there were no women around, save Indian women. Well, Charlie's mother was a maiden of great worth because she not only had two children by this early day settler who later married in full legality, the daughter of a lovely white family that came west and had another family that was housed and still lives in the Descendants do down in Lewiston, and they're a distinguished family, and I shall not identify any further. But this Indian woman was put aside when her husband could fell in love and could marry this lovely white girl. It this silly story was told, and I don't believe it for a minute, but it does carry a certain punch that when the Indian woman didn't get proper care or had a disagreement, she just Moses down the river with a teapot and pitched it in the yard of the white woman, and things came her way. But quickly now I think that's just a good story, but it is a funny one.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: The imagery is very good, isn't it? Well, Charlie was one of two of her children. Later she married an Army man who was a very early settler and had another child by him. And I don't think it hurts to tell his name because his father was Tom Bell, BA L.L. there's a creek named for him down between and Spaulding. And his wife was this same Indian woman. Now, I found Indians in their adjustment to our culture. In my own way, in working with PTA, there was an Indian woman who was the president of the unit of PTA when I was district president and going around and visiting all the units, and she was a high class Indian woman, but she simply couldn't get to meetings on time. 40 minutes late was power. She had her problems. Her little daughter, whose picture is still a lovely thing on the postcards as emblematic of beautiful Nez Perce maiden hood broke her mother's heart by running away and going up during high school days and going up to Curt Lane with her boyfriend and and distressing her family. No. And her son came to the university and pledged Tall Kappa Epsilon and was a star basketball player for some time, but he didn't get a degree.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: He married well. However, he married the daughter of a fine white family from up the roadways, who nearly nearly died at the match. But in due time, this young Indian man was in a bar and someone provoked his anger and he took a swing at them. Well, the fella didn't like being swung at and he swung back and hit this chap square on the tip of his chin. The fellow fell backward, dead. He'd been hit by a trick punch, which is known to boxers. And the man who finds this funny pedal fatal is called a man with a glass jaw. And my friend's son was dead. There was a grandchild. And at the funeral of this young man, the white in-laws came. And that was the first time that the Indians and whites had mixed. Now this is within the last ten, 15 years. So the amalgamations and the melding of races and the assumptions of cultures is still a very living struggle.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Now let's see what other Indians have I known that are colorful. I told you about Reuben and Connor and Peel. Bill. Yeah, I think those are the main ones. There were. There are other stories regarding the scarcity of women. Another man who was a big time cattleman and who has descendants living so I shan't name him, had a mistress named Brucie Jack. He took her out of a house, and she had had smallpox at some time when her face was greatly popped. So they said she was brocket, which I guess means deeply freckled or speckled. And Brockie. Jack was a very conscious of her lack of status. We had some friends named Vernon who were very proud people, and Mrs. Vernon could. So. So Brockie, Jack, the story goes, would bring a pattern, material thread. ET cetera. And put it on the post of the gate, and right away Mrs. Vernon would then come out of her house, go down and get the dress makings, and take them inside, and in due time produce a garment. I suppose there was a note accompanying regarding measurements, or maybe the pattern sufficed, I don't know.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: But then the garment was placed on the gate post, and Rocky Jack drove back, or rode back and picked up the dress, so there never was any personal contact. This, I imagine, was a matter of pride with Rocky Jack quite as much as Mrs. Vernon. She was not about to be snubbed, and I don't think, knowing Mrs. Vernon that she would have been. Now there's another Indian story that I wish to tell because it hurt me to see this happen. Jane Silcott was the daughter of Chief Timothy, and she was married to Silcott Captain Joseph Silcott, who was a snake River boat captain. He she died and he buried her where her grave could look up the snake River at the confluence of the snake and the Clearwater rivers. For many years that tall white shaft was there, and we all thought of the fact that here was a white man who had loved his wife enough to mark her grave in such a knowing way.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: But in due time, vandals came and ripped down the monument and desecrated the grave. And now there is nothing but harvested wheat over the place where this touching monument used to be. I have another story about Jane Silcott and her husband. A friend of mine was a 17 year old boy and working around the Raymond House, an early day hotel in Lewiston. And from the train one day came a distinguished lady from the East who said she had come to visit her brother, Joseph Silcott. Well, word was sent to Joseph Silcott and he came in all dressed up and took his sister to dinner. He took her for rides. He entertained her as royally as Lewis and afforded for several days, and then put her on the train and sent her home, never having taken her back to his house or having introduced her to his wife. Whether she ever knew that he was married to an Indian woman is quite doubtful, because she was made happy as could be. And perhaps there was never a question in her mind.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Now let's see what other stories I have here. Oh, when I was a little girl during World War one, and we were busy growing food for those big brothers overseas, I trapped squirrels. I got a nickel a tail and a dime for a weasel, and lots of times I got snakes. I never got a rattler, though, and I learned that I could take a little scout size ax and dispatch the squirrels, protesting and fighting for their little lives in those cotton. Those traps that I could pull them by their poor little court legs from where they'd frantically try to stay down the hole, that I could pull them forth and dispatch them. Because, after all, I had to preserve the food for our boys. This was a terrible lesson for a little girl to have to learn. But learn that I did. I took the money from those squirrels tails and sent to Sears Roebuck and got myself a ukulele. I learned four chords and oh why? If I couldn't sing up a storm with that instrument. And we also had an old fashioned phonograph that the parents my parents had bought at Bell's store in Genesee. This store had formerly been owned by Mark means and oh my, it had such delightful records as as Josh, uncle Josh and the Chinese Laundry and the.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Harrigan. And later I found it, my grandparents, The Whistler and His dog, and in the Good old summertime. And then, having learned to play this instrument, I went over to the Rubin Ranch, and somebody had left there another phonograph, and on it was the whole damn family. I'd be damn, and you be damn and the whole damn family. And when it came time for me to go to town and be sent Sunday school and eventually to school, my mother told me very carefully not to sing all down. And Tucker was a good old man, washed his face in a frying pan, combed his hair with a wagon wheel, and died with a toothache in his heel. Because I said old Dan Haley did this. And Dan Haley was a very respectable and well-liked neighbor. But the Iron Man had taught me the wrong words. And so, of course, on IV dam song, I didn't dare sing that for Sunday school. And it was very hard not to, because they wanted verses and they wanted songs, and those were the only ones I knew. Can you imagine how my mother was discovered wondering what I'd do about this time?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: We got the telephone. Now, I hadn't gone to school yet. When we'd got the telephone and the wire came from a racked up pole, and the pole was just anchored so carefully with great boulders at the corner of our field. And then that heavy wire swung clear across that vast canyon, over to the other side, to another well anchored pole. There were 19 people on our wire, and our ring was too long and two shorts, and it didn't ring at night, or it didn't ring for long distance unless there was death or disaster. However, one time it did, it rang at night and my father got up and I can see him yet in his long underwear, and all of us children dashing out of it downstairs to find out what terrible thing had happened. Well, it was long distance from Spokane, and the young man was the son of Reverend Stephen Rubin. His name was Lewis. And he said, Walter. Yes, this is Lewis Rubin. Yes. I am incarcerated in the Spokane County jail. You are? What'd you get in trouble bout? Well, they have me here now, Walter. You know I'm the original Aborigine.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yeah, I know that. Well, you know, they have me incarcerated me for introducing liquor on the Nez Perce reservation, and I need $20. Walter, I needed soon. Well, of course, any landlord is from time to time to have a little goodwill and public relations. Demand a little cash from time to time. But $20 was enormous. That was the equivalent of a steer. Oh, I don't remember how my father solved this, but I do remember that my brother, who also rented from Indians several years later, had an amusing incident happened to him along these very lines. The. He's ten years older than I. He was renting this Indian land, and in the sixth grade he'd gone to school down at this country school on the Canyon Road with some girls. I think their name was Andrews or Alexander or something. I white name anyway. And there were two of them, and they were all in the same grade with Ben.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And here came years later, has grown people. They were landlady's and he was a prospective renter. He wanted to do business with them to ransom their land up the Clearwater out of Lenore. Well, the girls couldn't speak English. They had to have an interpreter. Well, of course, what it was, was they were given some of their boyfriends little chance to earn a little government money by interpreting why it was just normal. They'd learn white man's ways. They weren't dumb. And so they had the interpreter there. Well, all the business went on through the interpreter, but from time to time the girls would see Ben in Lewiston or players falling or someplace, and they'd hit him up for a little money. Well, he'd give them a few dollars. And goodwill remained at a high point. But this Sunday the family was having dinner. When rattled bang! Into the door yard came this old Ford with one of the girls and two quite high Indian bucks, and they were all having a very fine time indeed. And Ben left his dinner table and went down to visit with a minute. And this Indian said, pen, don't make a bee sound.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: It's kind of a p sound pen. Emily would like to have a little money and said, well, gee, she hit me up in Lewiston twice this week. That ought to be enough for now, I guess. Maybe not today. Oh, she would like it very much. Well, I tell you, it's getting a little heavy. And she looked across this very jovial driver and looked merrily at Ben and said, oh, Penn, give me a couple of books and I'll scram the hell out of here. After that, it just didn't hold. Have an interpreter. Ben's served at a completely pagan funeral while he lived on that place. He said that his neighbor died and he was not a Christian Indian. They hired Ben to bring his team and the running gears of his wagon down. And they just laid a couple of boards across these two sets of wheels. You see the running gears, and the old Indian was rolled in his blanket, his good hat, his quirt, his bridle, his saddle, his his saddle blanket.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Were all with him. And up the hill they went to where a grave was dug, and he was laid in the ground. The Indians are very faithful to honor their dead, and the house was full of friends and relatives. The women keyed absolutely, just as in the old days. And then my sister in law didn't go. But the widow of the dead man gave away everything in that house. And when she didn't have enough possessions to give away, she had some gifts at hand, like a dress length of new material or something. A dress length of of cotton material was sent to my sister in law, and Ben, for what he had done to help with the funeral, was given a beautiful pair of buckskin gloves, handmade by the widow, and when the day was over, she not only did not have her husband anymore. She had no possessions. Now that was a completely pagan funeral, and the only one I've known of the family having a part of. Most of the Nez Perce were converted by Reverend Spaulding and his followers.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And this has an amusing aftermath, which I not tell today, because I'm not going to get all this material in. Let's see, I had quite interesting experiences with hired men and bunkhouses. Being the daughter of the family and just a little kid, I was allowed free access everywhere, and I didn't go up into the bunkhouse too much because in the first place didn't smell good. Those unwashed socks weren't didn't leave exactly a pleasant aroma. And daddy sorry stories seed corn up there and the mice would get in in the winter and they have a distinctive over odor. However, up there I learned to braid three strand and four strand and six strand, and in the there were certain seasons when it was empty. Then of course it would be full and I was not encouraged to go near it, but it was right in connection with our house. The bunkhouse was connected to the house by a breezeway, which dad eventually built.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: A shed over and under the bunkhouse was a very fine cellar that he built, with walls oh 18in to 2ft thick, and this kept the cream nice and the milk and butter in in hottest, whether it was always in quite nice shape, properly provided for out in the cellar. The doors were double and triple and with their padding, and so the heat was kept out and the roof was the bunkhouse. The men's reading in the bunkhouse was of a variety that my mother didn't condone. Now, it wasn't that it was dirty or four letter words used because these men were not dirty mouthed people at all. But it was a cheap variety of magazine, like the Blue Book and the top notch and mostly western stories. Not especially well written though there was a lot of Zane Gray mingled in with it, which mother didn't recommend to me, but which she didn't forbid me.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: However, she forbade me to to have anything to do with Horatio Alger that was too cheap. Now, Gene Stratton Porter I met with great pleasure The girl of the Limberlost and the Harvester and so forth were very mild little love stories with lots of nature. Laura, that mother said, I could, could have. And I had a delightful time the last summer we were there. No, not the last summer, but one of the later summers, because we had a a hot blooded thoroughbred called Ruby, and she was such a high spirited animal that I couldn't write any place but within the field or within the door yard. They wouldn't let me out on the road with her because she could take the bittern and go. And so I rode her to keep the cows out of some grain. Dad had a strip of spring wheat that he kept the cows in good shape with, and there were 15 cows in one bull, and I heard them twice a day all summer on Ruby. And my dear delight was to get a top notch to read while I did my little chore.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And if by any chance I could have found a nickel and could have bought a Centennial chocolate, it was one chocolate in a box all by itself, and it had the most delicious cream inside, often with black walnuts sprinkled in the the delicious thing. And I would manage to make that last almost an hour until it melted in my hands and then read top notch and still be way and by myself like that and doing my work. Why, that was a combination beyond compare. Of course the stock were interesting. Father didn't encourage us to name animals because pretty soon they'd have to be butchered or sold or something. And it was it. Since they were part of the business, it wasn't well that children got their heart strings to attached. So we many times named the cows for the wife of the man who had sold it to us.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Now this made for amusing situations. All rosy got in the fence this morning. Oh, old many had her calf last night or something like this, and it was a source of amusement to us. A little cruel, but all right. We named this summer. I named most of the cows movie stars. There was Mary Pickford and Norma Talmadge and Colleen Moore and Pearl white, and just the whole gamut of people that I had had a nickel to go to the Saturday show and get acquainted with these names. We didn't know whether to name the bull Francis Bushman or Brigham Young, but both names, we knew who we were talking about when we when we and they and them and. That's mainly my story about cows. We've got lovely stories about horses. However, Prince was the first horse that I remember. He was a ball faced roan and the sweetest old clumber of a riding horse you ever saw. He practically raised my two older brothers and older sister. When the little fellas would get to fighting on his back and one would get pushed off, the remaining writer couldn't get Prince to budge till he had two fellows on his back, and that saved many a worry for mother, because she knew that the children wouldn't get in trouble because Prince would bring them home.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: There was another horse that we loved. Everybody loved. She was Madge and she was a white Appaloosa. We had bought her from a homesteader who had been snowed in on his claim, and had kept her alive through a winter by baking white bread. She would she could eat it. She couldn't eat flour, but she could eat white bread. And they kept her alive through the winter. So she was a dear, pink nosed, light eyed pet. And of all the people who were daughter, Ben was the main one. As a matter of fact, Ben gave me her bridle not five years ago, and I have it here in the closet to give to the museum when we get a barn section saying to me. Here's something, old Gracie. I never found another animal that was good enough to wear it. He as a sweet, tender hearted little boy aged about ten, fell in love with match and she was his horse and he tended her. He carried her. He babied her with special food and he loved her well. One day we went to town for Decoration Day and I'll speak of Decoration Days next.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And when we got home there was a little colt, a beautiful Appaloosa. But Madge was dead. She'd had her colt and she hadn't survived. And Ben told me when he gave her the bridle how terrible it had been for him to see Madge. With a rope put around her neck, and dragged out and down into the canyon, which was the burial she got, he said, just like so much garbage. How his little heart had bled. Now Prince was buried. He was buried under a thorn bush, and I can find it to this day. He was properly buried, but match was just dragged down over the edge of the canyon. I suppose dad was very busy, or he certainly would never have done this that way. And we never knew quite where a match was. But Princess Place is still marked. There were other horses, but a few of them that took the places of these two. I also have a story of a mad dog, a mad coyote. It was. People lived on the Reuben Ranch. This was before I can remember much about it, who were white named? Trautman and Mr. Trautman went out after the cow one morning and took his little boy with him.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: As they went out past a straw stack out past the end of the barn, which my father owned, to get the cow, a coyote in most unusual fashion, loped right up to that little boy and bit him in the cheek before the father could prevent it or anything, that child was bit in the cheek. Well, of course he dispatched the coyote and its mouth was frothing, and it obviously was infected by rabies. Well, they took the little boy who became violently ill to the Toomey hospital in my in Genesee, and he lived for some time, and the wound healed to a scab the size of a grain of wheat. A nurse later told me. And then he went mad and had a horrible death. Our dogs at that time were stubborn, Jack, and for the only times I've ever known that they were kept in at night and this was in the granary, was during that trying interlude. And when they were out, they were muzzled for fear they would infect another animal or bite one of us children, because if rabies were rampant, then of course every living creature was vulnerable.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: This was a very trying time as I remember it. It was only later that I talked to the nurse, who was a neighbor of ours, and learned about how close the little boy had climbed to healing in the course of the terrible disease. But of course, at that time there was no there was no Pasteur or the finding of serum or anything. Rabies were just fatal. And if you got it in a rat population or a squirrel population or a coyote population, you just had to live with it until you could dispatch all of the infected animals. Time does march on, and the world does get better in many ways. I remember when electricity came to our home, of course, we just had the center cord come down with a light on it, but mother did insist on some pretty shades, and she did use high powered globes, which many of our friends did not.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: But we were all readers in our family, and we had to have light. She felt, and we had a great deal of the classics, so that the fine print of the classic books had to be to be seen, to be read. And so she had the electric lights in that fashion. It was, I think, 1916 when we got electric lights, and the man who brought wired our house was named Walter, and he was very handsome, and he got a crush on my older sister, but it didn't last long. This was a great boon to us. And then mother wanted an electric washer. But how to get an electric washer? Well, she had another horse whom I should tell you about. His name was Follow Me. He had been trained by a dear friend named Jim Vernon to follow Mr. Vernon. Every place that Mr. Vernon went, as long as he carried a whip and follow me, was a large Clydesdale, in part so that it gave him large bones, and he was a about a 15 hands high horse.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Mother didn't have a fancy buggy. She had a utility four wheeled vehicle that she could take the cream can and the egg crate and the raspberry crate, or the cherry crate, and a couple of children and go to town with mother was always dainty and pretty, and she would get her face made up and her false switch on and her hat set just right, and her makeup wound up with cream to camellia. And oh, she smells so good. And she looked so pretty. And then she'd put on a duster to keep her costume nicely free of dust for when she got to town to do her training as a little girl. I also had a duster that I wore and oh, I remember getting all tricked out, ready to go to town, and then that dust along that dirt road would just get on my pretty little shoes and get on my duster. And when we'd get to town, we just have to be brushed and shaken thoroughly, and then we'd leave the dusters in the rig while we went downtown with our dresses as nicely starched and ironed as they could be after six miles or five miles, whatever it was of going through the heat town. The only thing was that I was a delicate child, and no matter what, I ate at my grandmother's at noon, if I had the slightest treat, like a little ice cream cone from the confectionery named Small's all the way home from the cemetery on, usually I would have to vomit and I would have a headache, and it was nothing but nerves.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: From the terrific excitement of getting to go to town. And to this day, I love the fact that we have closed warm cars for transportation because the weather can't keep us home and disappoint the very living daylights out of it like it did when I was a child. Well, mother was was a charming person, and she would entertain us with observations and human interest stories as we'd ride along. And since she was such a lonely person, she used his companions, the ears of her children, and we got all the law from her former home in Michigan. And when letters came, while we kept up with the births and deaths and marriages and engagements and everything else of her friends back there, and on these rides, when there'd be the time just to put in while the Follow Me took us to town and brought us home, why, she would just regale us with one delight after another.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Follow me, however, had a certain area where he was king and that was coming up a portion of the road which still is there, that has just a little flat pace as you climb, rather a steep hill in this flat place where a mother could easily put her foot on the brake and hold the buggy. Follow me. Knew that he'd get a little breather. And so when he would approach this flat place.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Bringing his swish, swish, swish would go his, tail back and forth, reminding that lady driver behind him that it was time. And so she'd put her foot on the brake and laugh and give him a rest. Many times as he switched to go forward, he would break wind mightily, and we would have to protect ourselves from anything that was liquid thereof, but a feel he would go and not expecting another rest. Then we got to our upper gate, and then whatever child was there. Not a little girl like me, but my little brother, four years older, was the one to open the gate and then down the road, past the locusts and the walnuts. We would go, and then we'd see the light of home and the smoke coming up chimney. And was that nice? Welcome, I tell you, that was that was home and love and comfort. And yet I can remember many times when we came home and all of us were alone. And so we came into a cold house. We had to light the lights. We had to go out and gather kindling and chips, come in and build a fire, heat water, water and mother make biscuits.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And it took quite a little while to get the comfort going. How simple it is these days and how different from them. My father was so sweet about his yard. Always. He planted lilacs. He planted yellow roses. He planted the climber rose. He planted trees for shade, and he tended them carefully out in the back near where my play box of sand was. And my little playhouse was, was a row of locusts. And in front of them. Oh, yes. And many syringes were a row of beehives, and we had our own honey. Mother didn't do outside work, and she didn't have her daughters out in the field or the corral or the garden either. It gave a girl a big risk to do heavy work, and she wasn't about to have her daughters in that realm of activity. Tall. And we never were hired girls to the neighbors. The neighbors came to our house, but never did we work for anyone else.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: This was her little pride. So when from time to time, mother would get all bundled up with a wide brimmed hat and a veil and one of dad's coats and long, long skirts and gloves and ties at the bottoms of her sleeves and ties around her neck. Neck, we'd know she was going to hive a swarm of bees. This she did often. And this she did well. And she never was stoned. This was fortunate because we learned much later that she was allergic to be steamed venom. This came about when she went back to the farm to help harvest the honey, and was just staying all night and dad's bachelor diggings, and they were working with the honey, and she had a five gallon kerosene oil tin, which dad had cut out the head of and had pounded down so that it was a lovely big, clean container. And they put the honeycomb in there and the the comb would stay on top and the, the honey would drain down into the bottom and all. It was simply delicious with her biscuits made of sour cream or sweet cream and served with homemade butter and that good honey, I tell you, it's ambrosia. Well, this particular time mother was washing a five gallon container and a dead bee was clung to the edge of the container from the year before use.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: As she swished her cloth and the suds of the water around to wash the container, her ring finger on her right hand came in contact with the stinger of that dead bee, and it stung her. She got the venom from the dead stinger, and she was so violently ill that dad had to cut her corset strings and bring her to town immediately, and she nearly died. So we were very none of the rest of us. We'd been stung a zillion, zillion times, but mother should never have been stoned. She just wasn't very well suited to the life she lived. Oh, yes, I did to her, out to the neighbors. It was after we lived in town. And what I did was make a layout for the lady next door, and she paid me for embroidering all the pretties on the babies, little nighties and sacks and and hugged me tight and all the little robes and things. I loved her and Broder.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I've got a hankie here that I crocheted on when I was four years old. I have a pillow top that I worked on when I was younger than that, though I don't know how much younger and when the big ladies would be doing their needlework. Well, of course I wanted to do. And I learned to knit and crochet and hard and do all the the things that I still enjoy doing. If I only had the eyes to do it all. Just like they did. So I printed that lay at and then I got. So I dusted and vacuumed for her, and she paid me $0.10 an hour for all of this. I was in high school, but that's not the lowest wage I ever got. In the early 20s I was night. Operator well, let's see, this was the summer of 1925. I was night operator of the telephone company. It was a local company, and my father was a director and was able to pull strings to get his daughter a job. I worked for $30 a month, and I went on Saturday night to take up my duties at 9:00, and I didn't leave the place until 7:00 Monday morning. The way the hours worked out, I made $0.09 an hour.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I was terrified in some aspects of this job because in a thunderstorm, every key there came down on that switchboard. And if a woman was expecting a baby and of course, everybody knew everybody, I'd get calls saying, Gracie, what time is it? Is it time to take my bread out? I the clock stopped or they would say, Will you look up in the bug catalog and tell me what's on page 11? I the page is missing or some such thing? Well, I remember one violent thunderstorm when the keys were just playing a dance on that keyboard. The switchboard. One of the older girls in high school was having a baby, and they called for the doctor, and I had to plug in on that lively switchboard, and I was terrified. I the electricity was off and I had to hand grind the ring, but I got her, the doctor, all right. My bed was pulled up right under the the protruding counter of the switchboard, and I slept right there in the daytime.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I made up the bed and pushed it over against the wall. The sanitary facilities were out back, and it was an unfinished attic that went clear up two stories, and bats inhabited that place. And at night, if I felt a call of nature, I was terrified of going out there to that constantly running little old toilet. And so I would suffer until daylight came, if possible. Otherwise, there were many fringe benefits in the job. I had a bowl who was across the street in the bank. Okay.
